author: dojo-community
name: streaming
context: Process large JSON inputs incrementally with --stream, reducers, JSON seq, and unbuffered output
info: This skill covers jq when document size or stream shape matters more than convenient random access. It uses `--stream` to emit path/value events, `reduce` and `foreach` to aggregate incrementally, `truncate_stream`, `fromstream`, and `tostream` to reconstruct or trim streamed data, and `--seq` or `--stream-errors` when the input is a stream of JSON texts or partially invalid records. Agents use this node for large logs, API exports, long-running event streams, and memory-conscious summarization.
triggers: process a large json file with jq
body:
# jq Streaming

Load this skill when the input is large enough that whole-document jq filters are awkward or memory-heavy, or when the source is a stream of JSON texts.

## Stream Shape

With `--stream`, jq emits arrays that describe paths and leaf values. Leaf events look like `[path, value]`, while closing events look like `[path]`.

~~~bash
jq --stream "." big.json | head
~~~

## Incremental Reduction

Use `reduce inputs as $item (...)` when you want one final summary from a stream.

~~~bash
jq -n --stream "reduce inputs as $item (0; if ($item | length == 2 and $item[0][-1] == \"duration_ms\") then . + $item[1] else . end)" metrics.json
~~~

## Intermediate Outputs

Use `foreach` when the reducer should emit intermediate states, not just a final answer.

~~~bash
jq "foreach .[] as $item (0; . + $item; {current: $item, total: .})" numbers.json
~~~

## Reconstructing Streamed Data

`tostream` converts a value into stream events, `truncate_stream` strips leading path components, and `fromstream` rebuilds JSON values from stream events.

~~~bash
jq ". as $doc | fromstream($doc | tostream)" sample.json
~~~

## Sequenced Inputs and Errors

Use `--seq` for `application/json-seq` sources and `--stream-errors` when malformed records should be surfaced as data instead of aborting the entire command.

For reusable reducers stored in files, continue to [[jq/modules]].

sections.title: Understanding stream events
sections.body: In `--stream` mode, jq emits arrays of path metadata and values instead of materializing the whole JSON document. Events with two elements represent scalar or empty-container leaf values, and events with one element represent the end of an array or object. Knowing this shape is the key to writing correct reducers.

sections.title: Incremental reductions
sections.body: Use `reduce inputs as $item (INIT; UPDATE)` when a large input should collapse into one count, sum, index, or summary object. This pattern keeps memory use predictable because each streamed event is processed and discarded.

sections.title: JSON sequence and long-lived streams
sections.body: Use `--seq` when the input or output is `application/json-seq`. Add `--unbuffered` when downstream consumers should see each result immediately. This is useful for long-running command pipelines where waiting for process completion is undesirable.

sections.title: Rebuilding values from streams
sections.body: `tostream`, `truncate_stream`, and `fromstream` let you turn structured JSON into stream events and reconstruct a whole value or subtree later. This is the pattern to use when only part of a huge document should be preserved as a normal JSON value.

links.context: Store complex reducers in files instead of inline shell strings
Stream-oriented jq commands are usually part of larger pipelines
Long-running jq streams often run as monitored processes
Switch back to query mode when the input size is manageable

related.note: Streaming depends on correct input flags and buffering behavior
Streaming jq commands are most useful inside pipelines
Large reducers are easier to maintain as jq program files

scripts.name: Stream Reduce
scripts.description: Run jq in --stream mode against stdin, a file, or inline input with optional JSON-seq and stream-errors flags

JQ_FILTER.description: jq filter to execute in stream mode

JQ_FILTER_FILE.description: Optional jq program file to use with -f instead of JQ_FILTER

JQ_STREAM_ERRORS.description: Use --stream-errors to surface malformed input as data

JQ_SEQ.description: Use --seq for application/json-seq input and output

JQ_UNBUFFERED.description: Flush after each output value with --unbuffered

JQ_RAW_OUTPUT.description: Write strings without JSON quotes via -r

JQ_VARS_JSON.description: JSON object whose keys become jq variables through --argjson

depends.reason: Streaming uses specialized jq flags such as --stream, --seq, and --unbuffered
The packaged wrapper runs in Bash
Streaming jq is typically chained with other processes
